fact: Two thousand people were in the Hyatt Regency lobby during the tea dance on July 17, 1981
source: National Bureau of Standards (NBS) Investigation Report, NBSIR 82-2465 (1982)

fact: The original single continuous rod design was changed to two shorter rods, which doubled the load on the upper walkway's connection
source: "Investigation of the Kansas City Hyatt Regency Walkways Collapse," NBS Report NBSIR 82-2465, Pfrang & Marshall (1982)

fact: 114 people were killed in the collapse
source: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), successor to NBS; widely documented figure

fact: It was the deadliest structural collapse in American history (at the time and until 9/11, which was an attack rather than an accidental structural failure — it remains the deadliest accidental structural collapse)
source: American Society of Civil Engineers (ASCE) case study; widely documented characterization

fact: The design change was not reviewed or rechecked by the structural engineers of record before construction
source: Henry Petroski, "To Engineer Is Human: The Role of Failure in Successful Design" (1985), Chapter 6
